lines.text: That's Trace — and it is not a chatbot you query.
It's an agent that watches your team, and speaks up the second you contradict yourselves.
Observe. Remember. Reason. Detect. Guard. Learn.
Here's a real engineering team — Sandesh, Ravindra, Pushpa and Ashwini — shipping fast across Slack and Discord.
Nobody is querying anything. Trace is simply watching.
Sandesh reaches for MongoDB on the new billing service. But months ago, Ravindra standardized every new service on PostgreSQL. Trace catches it instantly — cited, dated, and owned.
Then Sandesh pushes on-prem for a big customer. But the roadmap said cloud-only. Trace flags the reversal, and loops in Ashwini.
Pushpa starts a retry queue — one that Ravindra already shipped in platform-core. Duplicate work, caught before a day is wasted.
And when you do want to ask, it answers — with citations.
What did we decide about our database? It traces straight back to Ravindra's Q1 call.
And when there is nothing to say, it says nothing.
Underneath is one Cognee temporal knowledge graph.
When you drift, it walks the graph back to the exact decision, and pulls it into the light.
One graph-completion call retrieves the prior decision and judges the contradiction, in a single hop.
The money shot. A pull request migrates billing to MongoDB. Trace comments on the pull request itself, citing Ravindra's call. Decided in Slack — enforced on GitHub.
Here's the twist. In 2026, your code is written by a whole fleet of A-I agents that forget.
So before Cursor writes a line, it asks Trace: has the team already decided this? Postgres. Decided by Ravindra. Mongo, rejected. It corrects itself.
One endpoint. Every agent. The same company brain.
And it watches the people, not just the code. Authentication is owned by Ravindra alone — and he is on leave next month.
Ask what breaks if he is out, and every consequence is cited to a real message.
Trace. It observes, remembers, reasons — and it acts. Nobody has to ask.
Your team's memory — with a pulse.
